Claude.notes: Strong conceptual foundation with crisp definitions and accurate dated cases (ChatGPT Nov 2022, Mata v. Avianca, Thaler v. Perlmutter). The story scenes work as historical kickers but do not pose a problem the lesson resolves — the concept is told, not earned through a character's predicament. Each lesson introduces new historical figures rather than carrying a recurring narrative protagonist, which weakens the story-driven brand of the curriculum.
Claude.topIssue: Labs are pure chat-with-AI with a 3-exchange threshold; learners never actually use a generative AI image/text/code tool on a real creative task, leaving the 'capability delta' soft.

Gemini.notes: Definitions of RLHF, transformer, attention, context window, hallucination, and parameters are technically accurate at the right depth for middle/high school. GPT-3 175B parameter figure is correct; module appropriately notes GPT-4's parameter count is unconfirmed. Mata v. Avianca details (six fabricated citations) and Thaler v. Perlmutter framing are correct. The tokenization example ('Unbelievable' → un/believ/able) is illustratively reasonable.
Gemini.topIssue: GitHub Copilot '55% productivity' figure is from a single 2022 GitHub-authored study and presented without that caveat — readers may treat it as more settled than it is.

Perplexity.notes: Most cited tools (ChatGPT, DALL·E 3, Suno, GPT-4 Turbo, Claude 3.5) and dates are roughly current as of late 2024 but feel a vintage behind by mid-2026 — Claude 3.5 has been superseded multiple generations, and the 128K/200K context windows shown are no longer notable. The anchor incidents (Mata case, Thaler ruling) remain canonical. The chat-lab proxy hardcodes 'claude-opus-4-5' which is not a current public model id.
Perplexity.topIssue: Context-window comparison table tops out at Claude 3.5 / GPT-4 Turbo and reads as 2024-vintage; learners reading in 2026 will encounter much larger context windows in deployed tools.

modules.module: M2: What AI Does With Words

Claude.notes: WGA-strike, CNET, and Clarkesworld stories give the lesson real stakes and the 'three pillars' (clarity / context / constraints) frame is genuinely useful. The Critique Mode quiz item is one of the only places in the course that tests application rather than recall. Story-density issue persists — short historical paragraphs into long expository concept blocks.
Claude.topIssue: The lab still doesn't ask students to actually engineer prompts against a real AI image/text generator; they only describe three prompts to a chat tutor.

Gemini.notes: Most facts are correct, but the lesson dates Word2Vec to 2016 — Mikolov et al. published the original Word2Vec papers in 2013 (with code release the same year). The 'GPT-4 has an estimated 1.76 trillion parameters' figure is presented as fact but originates from an unverified leak; a more careful framing would mark it as estimated/unconfirmed. The CNET '77 articles, more than half with errors' framing matches the public audit; the WGA outcome (AI not 'literary material') is accurately summarized.
Gemini.topIssue: Word2Vec is dated to 2016 in the opening story scene — the actual papers (Mikolov et al.) are from 2013.

Perplexity.notes: WGA strike resolution, CNET case, and Clarkesworld closure are still the canonical reference points two years on, so currency is acceptable. The '$300K prompt-engineer salary' anchor was true in 2023 but has cooled significantly by 2026 as the role has been absorbed into broader 'AI engineer' titles — students reading today should be told the market shifted.
Perplexity.topIssue: The 'prompt engineer at $300K' framing is now a 2023-vintage data point; the role's compensation and even existence have evolved noticeably by 2026 and the lesson does not flag this.

Claude.notes: Strongest narrative module so far — Jason Allen / Théâtre D'Opéra Spatial, Higgins's fake-Trump experiment, and the Hong Kong deepfake CFO call each anchor a real, vivid scene that genuinely sets up the concept. Quizzes are 5 questions instead of 3, giving more room for judgment-style items. Diffusion analogy (ink-in-water reversed) is excellent for the level.
Claude.topIssue: Detection-checklist lab (Lab 3) has students describe images verbally to an AI tutor rather than analyze actual AI-generated images side-by-side — close to a real exercise but not quite.

Gemini.notes: Diffusion is described correctly as iterative noise removal guided by a conditioning prompt — a clean mid-school-level explanation that doesn't overstate. LAION-5B size, Stable Diffusion's August 2022 open release, and Adobe Firefly's licensed-data approach are accurate. Hong Kong deepfake CFO fraud (Feb 2024, ~HK$200M) matches the reporting. 'Latent space' framed as an internal map of concepts is a useful simplification.
Gemini.topIssue: Latent space is glossed simply as a 'map of concepts'; for an Advanced-leaning learner this skips the encoder/decoder distinction that actually defines latent diffusion.

Perplexity.notes: The detection checklist (hands, garbled text, glassy eyes) was reliable through 2023–2024 but is genuinely degrading: by mid-2026 frontier image models render hands and text correctly far more often. The 'hands are the most reliable tell' framing should be marked as a moving target. Andersen/McKernan/Ortiz suit is described as 'still ongoing as of 2024' which is accurate to write-time but stale to read-time.
Perplexity.topIssue: The 'check the hands' detection heuristic was reliable in 2023–2024 but is materially weaker in mid-2026 image models, and the lesson presents it as current best practice.

Claude.notes: 'Daddy's Car' and 'Heart on My Sleeve' are two of the strongest story scenes in the whole course — vivid, dated, and each opens onto the exact concept the lesson covers. Sony CSL / Flow Machines / 13,000 lead sheets, MuseNet (2019), and the Drake/Weeknd takedown are accurate. Ethics-debate lab is meaningfully open-ended.
Claude.topIssue: Lab 1 ('AI Music Explorer') is a chat lab with no actual music tool in the loop — students never generate, manipulate, or evaluate a real Suno/Udio output.

Gemini.notes: Flow Machines and the 'Daddy's Car' attribution to Benoît Carré is correct; MuseNet's 2019 OpenAI release and capabilities are accurately described. Heart on My Sleeve attribution to Ghostwriter977 and Universal's takedown action are correctly stated. 'AI was free and instant' as the explanation for temp-track substitution is a fair summary of what trade reporting actually said.
Gemini.topIssue: Lesson skirts the technical distinction between symbolic-music models (MuseNet, MIDI-based) and audio-domain models (Suno, Udio, Stable Audio); a learner could leave thinking these are the same kind of system.

Perplexity.notes: The Heart on My Sleeve / Drake-Weeknd / Ghostwriter977 incident is still the canonical reference point. Suno and Udio are name-checked but the lesson predates the 2024–2025 wave of label deals and lawsuits (RIAA v. Suno/Udio) — that's the more current story for a learner today. Daddy's Car / Flow Machines (2016) is genuinely a foundational reference and stays current as historical anchor.
Perplexity.topIssue: The 2024 RIAA lawsuits against Suno and Udio — the next major chapter of the AI-music story — are not mentioned, even though they directly extend the consent and copyright themes of the module.

Claude.notes: The strongest D5 module in the course. Lab 2 has students apply the four-factor fair-use test step by step; Lab 4 ('Design the Rules') asks them to draft policy and have it pushed back on. These are genuine evaluation tasks, not just chats. Greg Rutkowski opening is the closest the course gets to a story that creates exactly the problem the lesson resolves.
Claude.topIssue: The 'four-factor fair use' lab is excellent in concept but still routed through the chat assistant — the rubric/scaffold for learners to fill in their analysis would be more durable than a free-form conversation.

Gemini.notes: Greg Rutkowski case described accurately, including the 'in the style of' prompt phenomenon. LAION-5B figure (5.85B) is correct. Four-factor fair-use framing (purpose, nature, amount, market effect) is the actual statutory test under 17 U.S.C. §107 — accurate use of legal vocabulary for the level.
Gemini.topIssue: EU AI Act references are general; the module could note that the Act's training-data transparency requirements specifically apply to general-purpose AI models — the legal precision matters because policy literacy is a stated outcome.

Perplexity.notes: Greg Rutkowski case is still a touchstone but the Andersen/McKernan/Ortiz suit and NYT v. OpenAI suit have moved through significant 2024–2025 motions that aren't reflected. 'Have I Been Trained' is still active and relevant. The four-factor fair-use exercise stays useful regardless of how the active suits resolve.
Perplexity.topIssue: Module reads as 2023–2024 vintage on copyright litigation; a 2026 learner needs at minimum a one-sentence acknowledgement of NYT v. OpenAI, Andersen v. Stability progress, and the EU AI Act enforcement timeline.

Claude.notes: Hollie Mengert opening is the most personal story in the course — a single artist whose name became a model is exactly the kind of story-creates-problem narrative the rubric rewards. Labs 3 and 4 ask the learner to produce concrete deliverables (personal AI policy, voice-defense plan) — these have a clear capability delta. The 'creative signature mapping' exercise in Lab 1 is one of the few labs that puts the student in the protagonist seat.
Claude.topIssue: Lab outputs are still mediated by the chat assistant; without a downloadable template or worksheet, the personal AI policy never leaves the conversation.

Gemini.notes: Hollie Mengert / Hollie+ / Invoke AI details are correct including the platform attribution. WGA strike — '148 days, longest since 1988' — checks out (May 2 to Sep 27, 2023). Zarya of the Dawn / Kashtanova / partial-rescission summary correctly captures the Copyright Office's reasoning.
Gemini.topIssue: The 'opt-out vs opt-in' policy distinction is summarised cleanly but the technical mechanics of how Stable Diffusion / LAION opt-outs actually work (image hash registration via Have I Been Trained) could be explicit for learners forming a defense plan.

Perplexity.notes: The Mengert and Rutkowski-style cases remain emblematic. By 2026 the more current 'protect your voice' tooling is Glaze and Nightshade (style-cloaking adversarial perturbations from the Zhao lab at Chicago) — students drafting a defense plan today would expect at least a mention. The defense-plan lab is concretely useful regardless.
Perplexity.topIssue: Glaze and Nightshade are absent — they are the actual, currently deployed defensive tools that the 'voice defense plan' lab should reference for a 2026 learner.

modules.module: M7: AI as a Creative Partner

Claude.notes: Three-mode framework (Amplifier / Provocateur / Co-Author) is the most pedagogically sharp framing in the course — gives the learner real choices about how to relate to the tool. Sunspring (Oscar Sharp / Benjamin), Allen / Théâtre, Heart on My Sleeve, and Zarya of the Dawn are all genuine reference points. Lab 2 (Prompt Workshop) is closer to actual prompt practice than most labs but still mediated through chat.
Claude.topIssue: Sunspring is described as premiering in September 2016; the film actually premiered at Sci-Fi London in June 2016 — small but worth fixing in a story-anchor.

Gemini.notes: The four-element prompt framework (Subject / Style / Constraint / Perspective) is a defensible simplification of what the literature actually recommends and is internally consistent. Sunspring is mis-dated to September 2016 — the actual premiere was Sci-Fi London 48 in June 2016. Allen / Midjourney details and Kashtanova ruling specifics are correct.
Gemini.topIssue: Sunspring premiere date is given as September 2016 in the story scene; it premiered at Sci-Fi London 48 in June 2016.

Perplexity.notes: Amplifier/Provocateur/Co-Author framework holds up for any current model — the meta-skill survives model churn. Concrete tool callouts (Midjourney, ChatGPT, Suno) are fine but the 2026 creative-partner conversation increasingly involves agentic tools (Claude with computer use, multimodal Gemini) and the module is silent on the agentic shift.
Perplexity.topIssue: Module frames the creative-partner relationship in pure prompt-and-response terms; by 2026 the more current frontier is agentic / tool-using AI working over a creative project across many turns.

modules.module: M8: The Future of Creative Work

Claude.notes: Closing module lands well: WGA, Zarya of the Dawn, Heart on My Sleeve, and the Adobe / College Board partnership give a four-corner map of how AI is reshaping creative careers. Lab 4 (build a 3–5 year plan) is the clearest capability-delta lab in the entire course — a learner walks away with a real artifact.
Claude.topIssue: The Adobe / College Board partnership is described in confident specifics ('integrated AI literacy into AP Art and Design curricula') that are looser than the public record actually supports — the partnership exists but the framing should soften.

Gemini.notes: WGA outcome and Zarya-of-the-Dawn ruling are correctly summarised. The Adobe / College Board / 'AP Art and Design' framing is the weakest factual claim in the module — the public record shows Adobe Express integrations and AP partnerships but not specifically a curriculum integration of 'AI literacy' into AP Art. Khanmigo and MIT Media Lab references are accurate but vague.
Gemini.topIssue: The Adobe / College Board partnership is described as 'the first time a major standardized curriculum explicitly addressed AI tools' — that specific claim is not well-supported by the public record and should be sourced or softened.

Perplexity.notes: Goldman Sachs '300M jobs' figure was the headline 2023 estimate; many follow-on studies have revised both up and down. WGA terms are still in force. By 2026 the more current 'future of creative work' story includes agentic creative pipelines, video generation (Sora, Veo) and the early effects of these on shorter-form commercial work — none of which appear in this otherwise forward-looking module.
Perplexity.topIssue: Closing module on 'future of creative work' doesn't mention generative video (Sora, Veo, Runway Gen-3) which by 2026 is the most disruptive creative-jobs story in motion.
